hard.next_step: Bailey: Send follow-up to Axis Pipe summarizing Zycus' ability to support a combined S2P + Supplier Risk Management scope, and coordinate a deeper discovery / tailored demo. Axis Pipe prospects: Confirm whether they want one combined RFP or separate RFPs, share high-level requirements, timeline, stakeholders, and SAP integration expectations.
hard.ais_why: Prospect showed clear interest in a unified S2P + Supplier Risk platform, but there was no explicit discussion around AI-specific capabilities, AI agents, automation intelligence, or GenAI use cases. AI interest is implied only through the need for risk monitoring, alerts, supplier insights, and process efficiency.
hard.competitor: Status quo (manual procurement in SAP, no automation)
hard.primary_competitor: Status quo (manual procurement in SAP, no automation)

north_star_verdict.headline: Two strong May discovery calls proved real procurement pain and clear product fit with two operations influencers, but Axis has gone silent ~53 days since 14 May, no procurement leader or budget owner is engaged, and the agreed buyer next step (confirm RFP scope, timeline, stakeholders, SAP integration) is undelivered.
north_star_verdict.math: 178 days to close (close 31 Dec 2026). Stage Qualified since 18 May 2026 (~49 days in stage, no progression). Last verified buyer touch 14 May 2026, ~53 days silent as of 06 Jul 2026 and approaching the 60-day cold threshold. Two discovery calls (6 and 13 May) built early fit; buyer-dependent commitment open for ~53 days. Not forecast-critical: close is 178 days out, not inside 60.
evidence: Two substantive discovery calls (6 May and 13 May 2026) established concrete operational pain: 95%+ of purchase requisitions converted PR-to-PO manually in SAP, no real-time tracking on critical zero-stock items risking production line stoppages, and chronic supplier delivery delays with no vendor accountability (Francisco Guereca and Mark Patton, 13 May 2026).

Product fit was validated on the 13 May 2026 demo of iSource, iContract, iSupplier and Merlin: Francisco Guereca said 'your tool seems to be a better fit... he and I both feel the same on it, that there's a great opportunity to make better' (13 May 2026).
Bailey Erazo delivered the requested demo videos and slide decks via a Showpad hub the day after the call (Task, 14 May 2026), so the ball is with the buyer.
Buyer silent ~53 days since 14 May 2026; the buyer-dependent next step (confirm combined vs separate RFP, requirements, timeline, stakeholders, SAP integration expectations) remains undelivered.

Single-threaded across two operations influencers only: Mark Patton (Director of Operations / Supply Chain Manager) and Francisco Guereca (IT Manager). No economic buyer, decision maker, or procurement leadership mapped.

Mark Patton explicitly placed the decision outside the room on 13 May 2026: 'you're not talking to anyone in our procurement team... we would definitely have to get our procurement team involved in something like this if we were to go forward.'
Stage has sat at Qualified since 18 May 2026 with no movement toward Formal Evaluation or Shortlisted.

metrics.narrative: Pain is confirmed and operationally specific but never quantified in dollars or time saved. Francisco Guereca stated on 13 May 2026 that 'more than 95% are doing manually. They place that PR into a PO manually, looking for a better price, looking for maybe 3 vendors,' and that lead times are guessed by 'manually run the reports to try to see the last time it came in.' Mark Patton described the cost of poor visibility on 13 May 2026: critical zero-stock items with no reliable delivery updates create 'potential line stopping because the lack of inventory.' No downtime cost, cycle-time target, or working-capital savings figure has been put to any of it, so the next move is to co-build a quantified value case with the MRO planner's own procurement-assessment data.
sources: Avoma call 13 May 2026: Francisco Guereca on 95%+ manual PR-to-PO conversion
Avoma call 13 May 2026: Mark Patton on line-stoppage risk from untracked critical items
Avoma call 13 May 2026: Francisco Guereca on manual lead-time estimation

economic_buyer.narrative: No economic buyer has been identified or engaged, and the evidence shows why: both contacts sit in operations, not procurement or finance. Mark Patton stated on 13 May 2026 'you're not talking to anyone in our procurement team' and framed the goal as building a proposal to take upward: 'he would be able to ask some questions and then also work with me on who we would take this to as a proposal' (6 May 2026). Budget authority therefore sits with an as-yet-unnamed procurement leader / approver, not with Mark or Francisco. Identifying and getting introduced to that budget owner is the single highest-leverage gap to close.
sources: OpportunityContactRole: both contacts are Influencers, neither Economic Buyer nor Decision Maker
Avoma call 13 May 2026: Mark Patton, procurement team not yet involved
Avoma call 6 May 2026: Mark Patton, would work out who to take a proposal to

decision_criteria.narrative: Criteria are emerging from the discovery, not yet formalized in an RFP. From the 13 May 2026 demo, the buyer's implicit evaluation criteria are: bilateral real-time SAP integration ('any information within Zycus... will be reflected in SAP vice versa'), multi-language support for the Monterrey plant (Spanish UI and PO translation), automated multi-vendor sourcing and PO placement to replace the manual process, supplier risk / performance tracking, contract management, and Microsoft Teams-native requisition approvals. A confirmed non-fit boundary was set twice by Bailey: Zycus is not an MRP and will not manage min/max or reorder points, which the buyer accepted ('we don't need the help on the max and mins'). The formal criteria will only firm up once Axis decides on a combined vs separate RFP.
sources: Avoma call 13 May 2026: SAP bilateral sync, Monterrey Spanish support, Teams approvals, supplier risk
Avoma call 6 May 2026 and 13 May 2026: Bailey Erazo, Zycus is not an MRP

decision_process.narrative: The process is undefined and buyer-owned. Reading the two-call sequence: Mark Patton opened on 6 May 2026 as a self-described exploratory contact ('I don't do the procurement part of supply chain'), then brought his MRO planner onto 13 May 2026 to add procurement depth. The stated path forward was informal: 'do a check in... let us mull it over a little bit, then we can get back to you,' followed by involving procurement 'if we were to go forward.' No approval chain, no RFP timeline, and no evaluation stages have been named. The open buyer decision on 'one combined RFP or separate RFPs' is the first process fork, and it is still unanswered ~53 days later.
sources: Avoma call 6 May 2026: Mark Patton bringing the MRO planner to the next call
Avoma call 13 May 2026: informal 'check in and mull it over' next step
SF Next_Step__c: confirm combined vs separate RFP

paper_process.narrative: No paper process exists yet, which is normal for a Qualified-stage new-business deal with no proposal on the table. No MSA, Order Form, SOW, procurement channel, or InfoSec review has been raised, and no contract-expiration forcing function applies. Visibility here will only come once an economic buyer is engaged and a proposal / RFP response starts, so it is not a current gate but a downstream one to scope after multi-threading into procurement.
sources: SF stage: Qualified, no commercial or contracting activity logged
Avoma calls 6 and 13 May 2026: no contracting or legal discussion

identify_pain.narrative: The pain is confirmed, specific, and owned. Francisco Guereca (MRO / procurement process owner) articulated the sourcing and supplier pain on 13 May 2026: automatic PRs but 95%+ manual conversion to POs, no supplier negotiation or evaluation discipline, no consignment to reduce working capital, and no vendor accountability for late critical items ('there has to be a compensation for the vendor to us for all the delays'). Mark Patton (Director of Operations / Supply Chain) owns the inventory-visibility pain: critical zero-stock items with no delivery-status feedback loop, risking line stoppages, plus manual lead-time reconstruction from historical reports. Both are genuine operational pains; the open question is whether they are severe enough to force a decision without a compelling event.
sources: Avoma call 13 May 2026: Francisco Guereca on manual sourcing, consignment, vendor accountability
Avoma call 13 May 2026: Mark Patton on critical-item tracking and line-stoppage risk

champion.narrative: Francisco Guereca is a developing champion who is now at risk of cooling. What has developed: he completed a full procurement-process assessment before Zycus engaged ('he did a full assessment on our purchasing area'), brought Mark Patton in to advance the conversation, actively validated Zycus' fit on 13 May 2026 ('your tool seems to be a better fit'), and requested demo materials to review internally. What is still missing: he holds no commercial authority as an operations/IT influencer, he has not introduced Zycus to procurement leadership or any budget owner, and he has gone silent for ~53 days. A champion who cannot reach power and has stopped responding cannot carry the deal, so securing him and getting a procurement introduction is the priority.
sources: OpportunityContactRole: Francisco Guereca, Influencer
Avoma call 13 May 2026: Francisco Guereca validating fit and requesting materials
Avoma call 6 May 2026: the MRO planner's full procurement assessment
No buyer reply since 14 May 2026

competition.narrative: There is no named vendor competitor. The buyer confirmed they do not use SAP Ariba ('No, we are not using Ariba', 6 May 2026) and named no Coupa, GEP, Jaggaer, or Ivalua. The real competitor is the status quo: 95%+ manual procurement run inside SAP with no automation layer, sustained by inertia rather than a rival. That makes this a no-decision risk, not a bake-off: if Axis never engages procurement leadership and never issues an RFP, the manual process simply continues. Zycus wins by making the manual status quo expensive and visible (line-stoppage downtime, manual PR-to-PO labor, working capital tied up in the warehouse) and by proving the SAP-integrated automation and supplier-risk value with a quantified case.
sources: Avoma call 6 May 2026: Mark Patton, 'we are not using Ariba'
Avoma call 13 May 2026: Francisco Guereca, 95%+ manual PR-to-PO process
SF Competitors__c: no named competitor logged

deal_movement.summary: Early momentum in May (two discovery calls, stage moved to Qualified, demo materials delivered) has flattened into a ~53-day buyer silence with the agreed next step undelivered.

Second discovery + capability demo call held (iSource, iContract, iSupplier, Merlin); fit validated by Francisco Guereca

Buyer silent since; buyer-dependent RFP-scope confirmation still open

competitive_position.summary: No named vendor competitor and SAP Ariba explicitly ruled out ('we are not using Ariba', 6 May 2026). The single strongest current threat is the status-quo no-decision: 95%+ of procurement is run manually in SAP (Francisco Guereca, 13 May 2026) and, with no procurement leader engaged and no compelling event, the buyer can simply keep managing manually and never buy. Zycus is the only vendor in the frame; the risk is drift, not displacement.

competitors.name: Status quo (manual procurement in SAP, no automation)
competitors.quote: More than 95% are doing manually. They place that PR into a PO manually, looking for a better price, looking for maybe 3 vendors. (Francisco Guereca, 13 May 2026)
competitors.how_we_win: Make the manual status quo expensive and visible: quantify line-stoppage downtime, manual PR-to-PO labor, and warehouse working capital, then anchor iSource automated multi-vendor sourcing, iSupplier risk/performance tracking, iContract, and Merlin AI against SAP's bilateral integration. Attach a compelling event and take a quantified proposal to procurement leadership.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Strong capability alignment across the S2P scope demonstrated on 13 May 2026, with one clearly bounded non-fit (Zycus is not an MRP) that the buyer accepted.

best_practice_check.summary: Early-stage win-strategy levers: reach power, force a timeline, and quantify the value so a proposal can survive procurement.
flags: Multi-thread into procurement leadership: both contacts are operations/IT influencers who deferred the decision to an uninvolved procurement team, so ask Mark Patton for the intro he offered.
Manufacture a compelling event: no buyer deadline exists, so tie the case to a cost/downtime clock or the manual process will drift indefinitely.
Quantify the value case using the MRO planner's own assessment data (line-stoppage downtime, 95% manual PR-to-PO labor, warehouse working capital) to justify a procurement proposal.

items.risk: At risk: no reply since 14 May and no reach into procurement or budget power; the champion may fade before advancing.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: Merlin / AI is under-positioned. Bailey noted 'AI powered' and 'AI agents baked into the software' on 13 May 2026, but no Merlin, I2O, or agentic procurement narrative was tailored to Axis, and the buyer never engaged on AI. The AI story is available but not yet landed with a named carrier.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AIS 7.0 / Moderate: the field reads AI appetite as implied only, and the calls confirm it. The buyer wants automation, supplier risk alerts, and process efficiency but never asked about AI agents or GenAI. Appetite is latent, not expressed, so the AIS score is fairly rated and Merlin is an educate-the-buyer opportunity, not a stated requirement.

items.detail: No economic buyer or procurement leadership engaged; both contacts are operations/IT influencers who defer the decision.
The main threat is status-quo inertia: 95%+ manual procurement in SAP with no compelling event to force a change.
No buyer-owned timeline or RFP decision; close date 31 Dec 2026 is a placeholder and the buyer next step is ~53 days overdue.
No budget owner identified and no price or ROI floated, so the $100k has nothing to anchor to when it reaches procurement.

confidence_signals.summary: Engagement cooled after a strong 13 May demo: the buyer asked to 'mull it over,' deferred to an uninvolved procurement team, and has not replied in ~53 days.
items: Two-way engagement dropped to zero after the 14 May demo-material delivery.
Decision explicitly deferred to procurement leadership that has never been met.

critical_signals.text: The 13 May demo landed on fit (Francisco Guereca: 'your tool seems to be a better fit'), but ended with the buyer wanting to mull it over and no procurement decision-maker in the room.
There is no rival vendor and Ariba was ruled out; the real threat is a no-decision, since Axis can keep running procurement 95% manually in SAP indefinitely.
No new stakeholders have engaged; the budget owner and procurement leadership who must approve any purchase have never been met.
No price, ROI, or savings model has been discussed, so the $100k is unvalidated against any quantified value for procurement to weigh.

deal_scores_evidence.summary: Two strong May discovery calls proved real procurement pain and clear product fit with two operations influencers, but Axis has gone silent ~53 days, no procurement leader or budget owner is engaged, and the only real threat is a no-decision on a manual SAP status quo.

win_position.text: Zycus is the only vendor in the frame and fit is validated (Francisco Guereca, 13 May: 'your tool seems to be a better fit'), but the deal sits with two operations influencers who own neither procurement nor budget, so there is no path to a decision yet.
No economic buyer or procurement leader has been met; Mark Patton said plainly on 13 May 'you're not talking to anyone in our procurement team,' so the people who would sign are unmapped.

The buyer has been silent ~53 days since 14 May with the agreed next step (confirm RFP scope, timeline, stakeholders, SAP integration) undelivered, so the deal risks fading before it ever reaches procurement.
Operational pain is concrete and owned: 95%+ manual PR-to-PO in SAP and no real-time tracking on critical items risking line stoppages (Francisco Guereca and Mark Patton, 13 May).

deal_momentum.text: Two discovery calls in May built early momentum, but nothing has advanced in ~53 days and the stage has sat at Qualified since 18 May.
Engagement is still a generic capability overview; no pricing, security, legal, or timeline conversation has started.
Bailey delivered the requested demo materials via Showpad the day after the 13 May call, keeping the next move with the buyer.

customer_commitment.text: Francisco Guereca completed a full procurement assessment, championed the fit on 13 May, and both influencers asked to 'do a check in' after reviewing materials.
Commitment is soft: the buyers wanted to 'mull it over,' deferred any decision to an uninvolved procurement team, and the check-in never happened.

deal_risk.text: Single-threaded across two operations influencers with no economic buyer, decision maker, or procurement leadership mapped.
No compelling event or buyer timeline exists; the main threat is inertia, with Axis continuing to run procurement manually in SAP and never issuing an RFP.
No quantified value case or price has been floated, so the $100k has nothing to anchor to when it reaches procurement.

customer_commitment.evidence: Positive fit validation but soft commitment, decision deferred to uninvolved procurement, and ~53-day silence.

deal_risk.evidence: Single-thread, no EB, no compelling event, status-quo no-decision threat.

Bailey suggested supplier information management, eProcurement, eInvoicing and eSourcing as a possible day-one phase on 13 May, but that is a rep phasing idea, not a buyer-driven scope reduction; the open buyer question is combined vs separate RFP, not a narrowing.

customer_preference.evidence: Francisco Guereca on 13 May: 'your tool seems to be a better fit... great opportunity to make better' — genuine positive lean, but no Zycus-specific commercial next step and a request to 'mull it over' with an uninvolved procurement team.

business_case.evidence: Qualitative pain only (95%+ manual PR-to-PO, critical-item line-stoppage risk, working capital in the warehouse); no cost, cycle-time, or savings figure quantified.
